people.name: Devin Cross
people.blurb: Keeps the data platform standing — the pipelines, schedulers, and clusters everything else runs on. Orchestrates batch and streaming jobs, models the warehouse so analysts trust the numbers, and instruments the whole thing so a broken feed pages a human before it corrupts a downstream table.
people.body:
Keeps the data platform standing — the pipelines, schedulers, and clusters
everything else runs on. Orchestrates batch and streaming jobs, models the
warehouse so analysts trust the numbers, and instruments the whole thing so a
broken feed pages a human before it corrupts a downstream table.

Thinks about reliability, cost, and blast radius first. Increasingly pulled into
the ML side — the model teams want reproducible training data and somewhere to
run jobs, and that infrastructure problem is squarely his.

people.name: Maya Okafor
people.blurb: Serves and adapts large language models for production — quantising checkpoints to fit the hardware, standing up high-throughput inference, and wiring retrieval so answers stay grounded in a private corpus. Spends a lot of time on the unglamorous middle: chunking, embedding, evaluating whether a retrieval change actually helped, and keeping latency inside budget.
people.body:
Serves and adapts large language models for production — quantising checkpoints
to fit the hardware, standing up high-throughput inference, and wiring
retrieval so answers stay grounded in a private corpus. Spends a lot of time on
the unglamorous middle: chunking, embedding, evaluating whether a retrieval
change actually helped, and keeping latency inside budget.

Wants to push past single-shot retrieval into systems that plan, call tools,
and check their own work — agentic loops on top of the serving stack she
already runs.

people.name: Priya Nair
people.blurb: Builds multi-agent systems — teams of models that divide a task, argue, and get reconciled by a supervisor. Designs the tool interfaces they call, the prompts that keep them on task, and the guardrails that stop a confident wrong answer from propagating. Ships most experiments behind a quick Gradio front end so non-engineers can poke at them.
people.body:
Builds multi-agent systems — teams of models that divide a task, argue, and get
reconciled by a supervisor. Designs the tool interfaces they call, the prompts
that keep them on task, and the guardrails that stop a confident wrong answer
from propagating. Ships most experiments behind a quick Gradio front end so
non-engineers can poke at them.

Drawn to problems where autonomous actors operate under hard constraints —
trading desks and other high-stakes, many-agent domains — and wants deeper
grounding in the quant and retrieval machinery those systems lean on.

people.name: Ryan Bell
people.blurb: Builds systematic trading systems end to end — pulling market data, pricing derivatives, constructing portfolios under risk limits, and routing the resulting orders to a broker. Most of the day is spent on the vol surface: calibrating models to option chains, hunting for mispricings, and stress-testing the book before anything goes live.
people.body:
Builds systematic trading systems end to end — pulling market data, pricing
derivatives, constructing portfolios under risk limits, and routing the
resulting orders to a broker. Most of the day is spent on the vol surface:
calibrating models to option chains, hunting for mispricings, and stress-testing
the book before anything goes live.

Comfortable owning the whole pipeline from data feed to execution, but the part
that scales worst is research throughput — every new thesis is hand-built. Keen
to hand more of the generate-and-test loop to agents, and to replace hand-tuned
signals with learned ones where the data supports it.

people.name: Sam Reyes
people.blurb: Turns models and pipelines into things people can actually use — dashboards, internal tools, and product surfaces. Cares about the last mile: fast pages, legible data visualisation, and interfaces that make a complex system feel obvious. Often the person who takes a research demo and makes it presentable.
people.body:
Turns models and pipelines into things people can actually use — dashboards,
internal tools, and product surfaces. Cares about the last mile: fast pages,
legible data visualisation, and interfaces that make a complex system feel
obvious. Often the person who takes a research demo and makes it presentable.

Fluent on the front end and comfortable enough on the back end to wire an API,
but wants to understand the model and agent layers well enough to design better
interfaces for them rather than treating them as a black box.
